specification.name: OpenUI Specification
specification.description: Implementation-independent Web UI framework specification derived from OpenUI5 public library catalogs and component metadata grammar.

roots.title: Library Catalog Root
roots.responsibility: Discover the public scope of libraries, dependencies, types, interfaces, controls, and elements.

roots.title: Metadata Grammar Root
roots.responsibility: Define the normative grammar for properties, aggregations, associations, events, visibility, renderer linkage, and drag-and-drop semantics.

secondaryEvidence.responsibility: Project the same public contract into machine-readable API metadata for tooling and consistency checks.
Add authoring-time semantics without replacing the runtime contract.
Verify the documented component contract with executable evidence.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.purpose: Define the scope of the OpenUI specification as an implementation-independent contract derived from OpenUI5 public metadata and library declarations.
requirements: The master specification is `/openui.json`; the markdown files under `spec/` are synchronized prose views of the same contract.
The normative scope is the public runtime UI framework contract: libraries, public symbols, component metadata, interaction semantics, and compliance evidence tied to that contract.

Scope discovery starts from `library.js` catalogs, while normative interpretation of component shape comes from metadata grammar such as properties, aggregations, associations, events, defaults, visibility, renderer linkage, and drag-and-drop declarations.

Machine-readable API projections such as `api.json`, design-time metadata, and automated tests are supporting evidence that refine, validate, or operationalize the contract without replacing the runtime metadata model.
Build-time-only artifacts such as `.library` files may help locate dependencies, documentation resources, and packaging context, but they do not define the runtime contract by themselves.

tags.name: single-source-of-truth
tags.meaning: The canonical specification record lives in `/openui.json`, and derived markdown or generator outputs must stay consistent with it.

tags.name: public-runtime-contract
tags.meaning: Only publicly declared libraries, symbols, metadata members, and documented interaction semantics belong to the normative framework surface.

tags.name: scope-discovery
tags.meaning: Library-level declarations such as `library.js` define what components, types, interfaces, and dependencies enter the specification scope.

tags.name: metadata-grammar
tags.meaning: Managed object metadata provides the formal grammar used to interpret properties, aggregations, associations, events, defaults, visibility, renderer linkage, and drag-and-drop semantics.

tags.name: supporting-evidence
tags.meaning: Artifacts like `api.json`, design-time metadata, and compliance tests support extraction and validation, but remain secondary to the runtime metadata contract.

formalDefinitions.definition: An implementation-independent specification derived from OpenUI5 public library catalogs and metadata grammar, expressed canonically in `/openui.json` and rendered into prose under `spec/`.
The externally visible framework surface formed by public libraries, symbols, component metadata, interaction semantics, and compliance-relevant behavior that implementations must preserve.
The process of enumerating which libraries, dependencies, controls, elements, interfaces, and types belong to the specification, primarily by reading `library.js` declarations.

The normative rule set that explains how component metadata is interpreted, including properties, aggregations, associations, events, default values, visibility, renderer linkage, and drag-and-drop declarations.
Secondary sources such as `api.json`, design-time metadata, and automated tests that corroborate, project, or validate the public contract without becoming the contract themselves.

usage: Use the introduction to determine whether an upstream artifact contributes to normative scope, supporting evidence, or build-time context before extracting details into later sections.
Use the distinction between scope discovery and metadata grammar when normalizing OpenUI5 concepts into implementation-independent specification entries or generator inputs.
Use the introduction as the baseline rule for rejecting private implementation details, undocumented runtime behavior, or build-only metadata as normative framework requirements.
implementationNotes: Implementations should load `/openui.json` first and treat the markdown documents as derived explanatory material, not a competing source of truth.
Extraction or generator pipelines should enumerate libraries and symbols from `library.js`, then interpret component structure through metadata grammar before consulting supporting evidence.
If supporting evidence conflicts with runtime metadata, prefer the public runtime metadata model and record the secondary artifact as validation or documentation context.

examples.title: Scope discovery example
examples.description: A library declaration that lists dependencies, controls, elements, interfaces, and types establishes which public symbols must be considered by the specification.

examples.title: Metadata grammar example
examples.description: A component metadata block that declares properties, aggregations, associations, and events defines the formal contract that later sections normalize into component, interaction, and state requirements.

examples.title: Supporting evidence example
examples.description: An `api.json` export or QUnit test may confirm how a public event or property is surfaced, but it cannot widen scope beyond what the public runtime metadata already declares.

sections.title: Design Goals
sections.purpose: Describe the outcomes a compliant web UI framework must enable.
requirements: Enable library-based discovery of the public UI surface through a stable catalog of controls, elements, interfaces, and types.
Define components with declarative metadata instead of framework-specific implementation details.
Preserve a portable public contract that different implementations, tooling pipelines, and generators can consume without depending on a specific renderer or runtime internals.
Support generator and automation use cases by keeping the contract machine-readable, hierarchical, and normalization-friendly.
Make component capabilities, composition rules, types, and interaction hooks explicit enough to support validation, documentation, and interoperability across libraries.

tags.name: discoverable-public-surface
tags.meaning: The framework must expose its public libraries, controls, elements, interfaces, and types through stable catalog metadata that tools can enumerate.

tags.name: declarative-component-contract
tags.meaning: Component shape and capabilities must be expressed as metadata, not inferred from private implementation code.

tags.name: implementation-independent-contract
tags.meaning: The specification must capture what compliant implementations provide without coupling that contract to a renderer, build stack, or framework-internal class design.

tags.name: generator-ready-structure
tags.meaning: The contract must stay structured enough for validation, normalization, transformation, and code generation workflows.

tags.name: interoperable-library-ecosystem
tags.meaning: Libraries and components must publish enough typed information to support composition, documentation, and tooling across package boundaries.

formalDefinitions.definition: The ability to enumerate the framework's public libraries, controls, elements, interfaces, dependencies, and types from stable library-level declarations.
A public metadata description of a component's properties, composition, relationships, events, defaults, and capabilities, independent of private implementation details.
A specification record that defines externally observable framework semantics without requiring a specific renderer, runtime architecture, or build pipeline.
A machine-readable, hierarchical, and normalization-friendly representation of the framework contract that can be consumed by tooling, validators, or code generators.
Typed component and library metadata that allows other libraries, applications, and tools to compose against the public contract without inspecting internals.

usage: Use the design goals to decide whether a proposed specification entry improves discoverability, declarative clarity, portability, or tooling readiness before treating it as normative.
Use the separation between library catalogs and metadata grammar to keep scope discovery independent from component interpretation during extraction or normalization.
Use the design goals as a filter for excluding renderer-specific internals, build-only structure, or undocumented implementation details from the canonical contract.
implementationNotes: Implementations should expose their public surface through stable library declarations so tooling can enumerate components, interfaces, types, and dependencies without source inspection.

Extraction and generator pipelines should preserve typed names, multiplicity, defaults, and public relationships in normalized JSON so downstream tooling does not need to infer them from runtime code.
Secondary artifacts such as `api.json`, design-time metadata, and tests should be used to validate or operationalize the contract, while public runtime metadata remains the authoritative source.

examples.title: Discoverable public surface example
examples.description: A library declaration listing dependencies, types, interfaces, controls, and elements makes the public framework surface enumerable without reading private files.

examples.title: Declarative component contract example
examples.description: A component metadata block that declares typed properties, aggregations, associations, and events makes composition and interaction semantics explicit without exposing implementation internals.

examples.title: Generator-ready structure example
examples.description: A normalized JSON contract can drive validation, documentation generation, and framework-specific scaffolding without requiring parser logic for renderer internals.

sections.title: Design Principles
sections.purpose: Capture the principles used to normalize OpenUI5 concepts into a reusable framework specification.
requirements: Prefer public library catalogs and runtime metadata over private implementation details, renderer internals, or undocumented helper code.
Separate scope discovery (`library.js`) from semantic interpretation (metadata grammar) and from secondary API projections such as `api.json`.
Normalize OpenUI5 concepts into canonical JSON without losing public names, types, multiplicity, defaults, visibility, or declared relationships.
Specify observable framework capabilities and constraints rather than prescribing a specific renderer, widget lifecycle, build pipeline, or source layout.
Treat design-time metadata, generated API projections, and automated tests as supporting evidence that can refine validation and documentation but cannot override the public runtime contract.

Require opt-in semantics for specialized behavior: renderer linkage, drag-and-drop support, accessibility relationships, and similar features belong in the specification only when they are publicly declared.
nonGoals: The design principles do not require consumers to parse private source files, infer behavior from renderer code, or reverse-engineer undocumented conventions.
The design principles do not standardize framework-internal class hierarchies, optimization strategies, or build-time packaging details unless those details are surfaced through the public contract.
The design principles do not treat convenience projections such as `api.json` as a replacement for `library.js` discovery or managed object metadata.

tags.name: public-contract-first
tags.meaning: Normative specification entries come from publicly declared libraries and metadata before any private implementation details or generated projections are considered.

tags.name: layered-evidence-model
tags.meaning: Library catalogs, metadata grammar, API projections, design-time metadata, and tests each have distinct roles and must not be conflated.

tags.name: lossless-normalization
tags.meaning: Extraction must preserve the meaning of public metadata when converting framework concepts into canonical JSON.

tags.name: implementation-agnostic-semantics
tags.meaning: The specification defines observable framework behavior without binding compliant implementations to a specific rendering or build strategy.

tags.name: declared-capabilities-only
tags.meaning: Specialized behavior enters the specification only when it is explicitly declared in public metadata or equally authoritative public artifacts.

formalDefinitions.definition: The rule that publicly declared library and metadata artifacts outrank private code paths, renderer internals, and generated documentation when determining normative specification content.
A source hierarchy in which `library.js` discovers scope, metadata grammar defines semantics, and projections such as `api.json`, design-time metadata, and tests provide corroborating evidence.
The transformation of framework metadata into canonical JSON while preserving public names, typing, multiplicity, defaults, visibility, and declared relationships.
Externally observable framework requirements expressed independently of any concrete rendering technology, lifecycle implementation, or source-code organization.
A framework feature or behavior that is eligible for specification only because it is explicitly exposed through public metadata or another authoritative public contract artifact.

usage: Use these principles whenever extracting a new section from OpenUI5: determine scope from `library.js`, interpret meaning from metadata grammar, then use `api.json`, design-time metadata, or tests only to confirm or operationalize the extracted contract.

When normalizing metadata into `/openui.json`, preserve authored identifiers, public types, multiplicity flags, defaults, visibility, associations, and events rather than replacing them with generator-specific abstractions.
If a behavior appears only in renderer code, helper utilities, or undocumented conventions, treat it as non-normative unless an authoritative public artifact also declares it.
Record specialized capabilities only when the public contract explicitly exposes them, so downstream generators and validators can rely on deterministic, opt-in semantics.
Use the layered evidence model to resolve disagreements: public runtime metadata remains authoritative, while projections and tests help validate completeness, examples, or compliance rules.
implementationNotes: Specification maintainers should keep discovery, semantic interpretation, and validation as separate steps so the canonical JSON can be regenerated deterministically from authoritative sources.
Normalization pipelines should preserve authored metadata vocabulary and cardinality instead of collapsing public concepts into renderer-specific or generator-specific internal models.

Supporting artifacts such as `api.json`, design-time metadata, and tests should be linked back to the same public contract entry they corroborate, making disagreements visible without changing the normative source.

examples.title: Layered evidence and precedence example
examples.description: A `library.js` declaration and component metadata define the normative contract for a form component; a matching `api.json` entry confirms that contract, while a private helper or renderer detail does not expand it.

examples.title: Lossless normalization example
examples.description: A normalized metadata record preserves a component's public property names, association types, aggregation multiplicity, and default values instead of replacing them with generator-specific shortcuts.

examples.title: Declared capabilities only example
examples.description: Drag-and-drop, accessibility relationships, or renderer linkage become specification content only when public metadata explicitly declares them.

examples.title: Conflicting evidence resolution example
examples.description: If a generated API projection exposes an undocumented member that does not appear in authoritative runtime metadata, the specification keeps only the publicly declared metadata members.

sections.title: Target Users
sections.purpose: Identify the intended consumers of the specification.
requirements: Application developers use the specification to discover stable component contracts, supported composition patterns, interaction semantics, and state behavior before building application features.
Designers and UX owners use the specification to align component behavior, layout, feedback, accessibility, and theming expectations across libraries and applications.
Framework maintainers use the specification as the compatibility baseline that implementation changes must preserve at the public contract level.
Generator and tooling maintainers use the canonical JSON contract to validate metadata, generate framework-specific scaffolding, and keep derived artifacts synchronized.
Documentation and compliance authors use the specification to produce consistent guidance, acceptance criteria, and evidence tied to the same public contract.

tags.name: application-consumer
tags.meaning: Application developers consume the specification to understand which public components, states, events, and composition rules they can rely on.

tags.name: design-consumer
tags.meaning: Designers and UX owners consume the specification to align interaction, feedback, accessibility, and theming behavior with the documented contract.

tags.name: implementation-consumer
tags.meaning: Framework maintainers consume the specification as a compatibility boundary for public runtime behavior.

tags.name: tooling-consumer
tags.meaning: Generator, validation, and documentation tooling consume the canonical JSON contract as structured input.

tags.name: shared-contract
tags.meaning: All target users depend on the same normative contract even when they view it through different audience-specific workflows.

formalDefinitions.definition: A consumer who builds end-user features by composing public components, states, events, and layout patterns exposed by the specification.
A consumer who uses the specification to align user experience semantics such as labels, feedback, responsiveness, accessibility, and theming expectations.
A consumer who evolves an implementation while preserving the externally visible contract defined by the specification.
A consumer who transforms the canonical JSON contract into validation rules, documentation, code generation, or other derived artifacts.
The single normative framework description that all target users reference, regardless of whether they consume it through prose, validation, or tooling workflows.

usage: Use the target users section to identify the primary audience for each later specification rule so the level of abstraction stays focused on public behavior rather than internal implementation details.

Use the section when adding or changing entries in `/openui.json` so the canonical JSON remains structured enough for tooling consumers while the markdown prose remains understandable for application and design consumers.

Use the section as a reminder that audience-specific documents, generated APIs, and design references are derived materials that must stay consistent with the shared contract instead of redefining it.
implementationNotes: Implementations should keep `/openui.json` authoritative so every audience consumes the same normalized framework contract even when different renderings or tools present it differently.

Documentation, validation, and generator pipelines should derive audience-specific outputs from the canonical JSON without changing the meaning of public components, metadata, or interaction semantics.

When a section is expanded for a specific audience, the added prose should clarify the existing contract rather than introduce renderer-specific, application-specific, or private implementation rules.

examples.title: Application developer usage example
examples.description: A public metadata block for a button gives application developers stable text, enabled state, and activation semantics to build against without reading private runtime code.

examples.title: Designer and UX owner usage example
examples.description: A component contract that declares validation state and labeling associations gives designers and UX owners a stable source for aligning feedback and accessibility expectations.

examples.title: Framework and tooling maintainer usage example
examples.description: The same canonical JSON contract lets framework maintainers preserve compatibility while generator and documentation pipelines derive implementation-specific outputs from the identical source.

examples.title: Documentation and compliance author usage example
examples.description: The canonical section lets documentation and compliance authors derive audience guidance, acceptance criteria, and evidence trails from the same shared contract.

sections.title: UI Concept Model
sections.purpose: Define the abstract building blocks of the framework UI.
requirements: The public UI surface is composed from libraries that publish named controls and elements as reusable building blocks.

Controls are standalone renderable components that provide presentation and interaction behavior; elements are reusable supporting units that contribute structure or semantics through a parent component.
Composition is expressed declaratively through public metadata, primarily aggregations that define owned child content, named regions, and allowed child types.
Associations complement aggregations by expressing non-owning references such as labels, descriptions, and other semantic relationships between UI objects.
Higher-level concepts such as pages, dialogs, forms, lists, toolbars, and actions are modeled as specialized controls and elements rather than as framework primitives outside the metadata contract.
nonGoals: The UI concept model does not prescribe a specific visual design, renderer implementation, DOM structure, or styling strategy.
The UI concept model does not require every public symbol to be independently renderable; supporting elements may exist only as composition units within parent controls.
The UI concept model does not infer normative composition rules from private implementation code when those rules are not declared in public metadata.

tags.name: library-published-building-blocks
tags.meaning: Libraries publish the controls and elements that define the reusable public UI surface.

tags.name: control-element-distinction
tags.meaning: Controls are standalone renderable units, while elements are subordinate reusable units composed through parents.

tags.name: metadata-declared-composition
tags.meaning: Parent-child UI structure is normative only when declared in public metadata, especially through aggregations.

tags.name: owned-ui-content
tags.meaning: Aggregated children belong to the parent component's public composition contract and lifecycle.

tags.name: named-ui-regions
tags.meaning: Aggregations may define semantic regions such as header, content, footer, items, or actions without prescribing renderer internals.

formalDefinitions.definition: A public control or element that participates in the framework's reusable UI surface and can be discovered from library metadata.
A public component type intended to represent a standalone renderable UI unit with presentation and, where applicable, interaction behavior.
A public reusable UI unit that contributes structure, semantics, or configuration to a parent component without needing to stand alone as a renderable surface.
A parent-owned composition relationship declared in metadata that specifies child types, multiplicity, and semantic role within the UI.
A semantically named composition area, such as `header`, `content`, `footer`, or `items`, represented through an aggregation in public metadata.

usage: Use this model to classify public symbols as controls or elements before applying more specific rules from component, layout, form, interaction, or accessibility sections.
Model owned UI structure through aggregations and reserve associations for cross-references that do not transfer ownership, such as labels, descriptions, or semantic links.

Describe higher-level constructs such as pages, dialogs, forms, and list rows as compositions of controls and elements connected through named regions rather than through renderer-specific DOM assumptions.

When normalizing metadata into `/openui.json`, preserve aggregation names, child types, multiplicity, and semantic roles so tooling can reconstruct the UI concept hierarchy without reverse-engineering runtime code.
implementationNotes: Implementations should publish controls and elements through stable library catalogs so applications, validators, and generators can enumerate the available UI building blocks without source inspection.
Public metadata should declare aggregations with typed child contracts, multiplicity, and semantic naming so parent-owned composition can be interpreted without relying on renderer internals.

Extraction and generator pipelines should reconstruct high-level UI concepts from library catalogs plus composition metadata, while treating associations as semantic links rather than ownership boundaries.

examples.title: Library-published building blocks example
examples.description: A library declaration that lists page, button, and form controls together with form container elements makes the public UI surface discoverable as reusable building blocks.

examples.title: Named composition regions example
examples.description: A page control that declares `header`, `content`, `footer`, and `actions` aggregations exposes its semantic UI regions directly in public metadata.

examples.title: Form hierarchy example
examples.description: A form control that owns container and form-element children, while individual fields and label associations remain explicit, demonstrates how controls and elements combine into higher-level UI concepts.

examples.title: Dialog composition and semantic references example
examples.description: A dialog control that declares owned `content`, `beginButton`, and `endButton` aggregations while keeping `ariaLabelledBy` and `ariaDescribedBy` as associations demonstrates the difference between composition ownership and semantic cross-reference.

sections.title: Application Structure
sections.purpose: Describe the required structure of applications built on the framework.
requirements: Applications compose pages, dialogs, forms, lists, and navigation containers from library-published components rather than from framework-private primitives.
The framework must support a navigable shell-level structure and a page hierarchy built entirely from public components discoverable through the library catalog.
A shell is the top-level application container that hosts global navigation and a content area into which pages and nested containers are placed.
Page hierarchy is expressed through public composition metadata so that parent pages, nested pages, and embedded containers can be resolved without reading component internals.
Library dependencies must be explicit so that the application structure can be resolved by reading the dependency declaration and the public component catalog alone.
Every component referenced by the application structure must resolve to a public control or element published by a declared library dependency.
nonGoals: The application structure does not prescribe a specific routing engine, URL scheme, history strategy, or renderer for the shell and its pages.
The application structure does not require a fixed number of regions, navigation levels, or page depth; those are determined by the composed components and their public metadata.

The application structure does not infer the application graph from private wiring, build configuration, or runtime-only behavior that is absent from the public component catalog and explicit dependency declaration.

tags.name: library-composed-application
tags.meaning: Applications are assembled from controls and elements published by declared library dependencies, not from framework-private primitives.

tags.name: shell-level-structure
tags.meaning: A top-level shell container hosts global navigation and a content area that frames the application's page hierarchy.

tags.name: page-hierarchy
tags.meaning: Pages and nested containers form a parent-child hierarchy expressed through public composition metadata.

tags.name: explicit-library-dependencies
tags.meaning: Every library that contributes components to the application structure is declared explicitly so the structure resolves without source inspection.

tags.name: public-component-resolution
tags.meaning: Every structural node references a public control or element resolvable from a declared dependency's catalog.

formalDefinitions.definition: A composition of library-published controls and elements arranged into a navigable shell and page hierarchy that resolves entirely from public metadata and explicit library dependencies.
The top-level application container responsible for hosting global navigation and a content area into which pages and nested containers are placed.
A navigable structural unit, modeled as a public control, that frames primary content and may contain nested pages, regions, and containers.
A public component, such as a shell, split container, or tabbed container, whose aggregations hold the pages or views between which the application navigates.
An explicit declaration that an application or library relies on another library, making that library's public component catalog available for structural composition.

usage: Resolve the application structure by reading the explicit library dependency declaration first, then enumerating the public controls and elements those libraries publish, and only then composing the shell and pages from that catalog.

Model the shell as a navigation container whose aggregations hold pages and nested containers, and express each region (such as navigation, header, and content) through named aggregations rather than renderer-specific DOM assumptions.
Build the page hierarchy from controls and elements connected through aggregations, reserving associations for non-owning references such as the currently selected page or an initial page.

Keep library dependencies explicit and minimal so that tooling, validators, and generators can reconstruct the full application graph from the dependency declaration plus the public component catalog without reverse-engineering runtime code.

When normalizing the structure into `/openui.json`, preserve dependency names, container aggregations, child types, multiplicity, and the semantic role of each region so the navigable hierarchy can be rebuilt deterministically.
implementationNotes: Implementations should publish navigation containers, shells, and pages through stable library catalogs so applications, validators, and generators can resolve the structure without source inspection.

Applications should declare their library dependencies explicitly and reference a single root component so the full application graph can be reconstructed from the dependency declaration plus public metadata.
Extraction and generator pipelines should rebuild the shell and page hierarchy from container aggregations while treating references such as the current or initial page as non-owning associations.

examples.title: Explicit library dependencies example
examples.description: An application declaration that lists its library dependencies and names a single root component makes the structure resolvable from the dependency catalog without inspecting component internals.

examples.title: Shell-level structure example
examples.description: A shell control that declares `navigation`, `header`, and `pages` aggregations while keeping `currentPage` as an association exposes global navigation and owned pages directly in public metadata.

examples.title: Page hierarchy example
examples.description: A page control that owns content and nested `subPages`, together with a split container that holds master and detail pages, demonstrates how the navigable page hierarchy is expressed entirely through public aggregations.

examples.title: Resolved application structure tree example
examples.description: A resolved structure tree that combines the shell, navigation, pages, and nested containers shows how every structural node references a public component from a declared library dependency.

sections.title: Layout System
sections.purpose: Describe the abstract layout and composition requirements.
requirements: Layout is expressed as ordered or named composition regions exposed through aggregations.
Responsive behavior must preserve component composition semantics when layout density, theme, or viewport changes.
Container components may expose drag-and-drop semantics only when declared in metadata.
nonGoals: The layout system does not prescribe a specific CSS technique, grid engine, DOM structure, or runtime framework; grid, flexbox, and absolute strategies are all permissible implementations of the same abstract regions.

The layout system does not define a fixed set of breakpoints, density levels, or spacing values; it requires that they exist as a consistent, theme-driven scale, not that they take specific pixel measurements.

The layout system does not introduce a new composition mechanism: regions are aggregations from the component model, and the layout section only constrains how those aggregations are arranged and adapted.
The layout system does not make drag-and-drop a default capability; reordering and cross-region moves are available only where a container explicitly declares them in metadata.

tags.name: composition-region
tags.meaning: Layout is expressed as ordered or named regions, each backed by an aggregation whose name and multiplicity define placement and capacity.

tags.name: ordered-content
tags.meaning: Children of an ordered region have a normative document order that defines their visual order before responsive adaptation.

tags.name: responsive-behavior
tags.meaning: Region arrangement may reflow, stack, wrap, or hide across breakpoints, density, and theme changes while preserving composition semantics.

tags.name: breakpoint-model
tags.meaning: Named viewport ranges at which a layout may adapt its arrangement without changing owned content.

tags.name: layout-density
tags.meaning: A layout-wide modifier that scales spacing and sizing without changing which regions or children apply.

tags.name: spacing-scale
tags.meaning: Spacing between and within regions is drawn from a consistent, theme-driven scale rather than ad-hoc values.

tags.name: container-dnd
tags.meaning: Drag-and-drop reordering or cross-region moves are available only when a container declares them in metadata and stay within the aggregation contract.

formalDefinitions.definition: The arrangement of a container's composition regions and their content across the available space, including its responsive, density, and spacing behavior.
A named or ordered placement target within a container, backed by an aggregation, into which owned child content is placed.
A region whose backing aggregation is a 0..n collection in which document order is the normative visual order before responsive adaptation.
A region addressed by a stable name (such as header, content, or footer), typically backed by a 0..1 or 0..n aggregation, into which content is placed by referencing that name.
A named viewport range that bounds a responsive adaptation; crossing a breakpoint may change arrangement but not owned content.
A layout-wide modifier that scales spacing and control sizing uniformly without changing the set of regions or their children.
The consistent, theme-driven set of spacing steps used between and within regions instead of ad-hoc values.
The rules by which regions reflow, stack, wrap, or hide as viewport, density, or theme change while preserving composition semantics.
A region whose container declares drag-and-drop in metadata, allowing children to be reordered or moved within the declared aggregation contract.

usage: Model every layout as a set of aggregation-backed regions before choosing a rendering technique; name the regions that carry meaning (such as header, content, aside, footer) and reserve ordered 0..n aggregations for collections whose sequence matters.

Treat document order within an ordered region as the source of truth: derive the visual order from the aggregation, and let responsive rules change arrangement without re-sorting or reparenting the underlying children.

Express responsive adaptation as breakpoint-driven arrangement changes (reflow, stacking, wrapping, or hiding) that leave the region set and its owned content intact, so the composition tree stays resolvable from metadata at every viewport.

Apply density and theming as layout-wide modifiers sourced from the spacing scale and design tokens, rather than hard-coding per-component spacing, so density and theme changes preserve alignment and composition.

Declare drag-and-drop only where reordering or cross-region moves are part of the contract, and constrain it to the target aggregation's child type and multiplicity so a drop can never violate the component model.

When normalizing a layout into /openui.json, preserve each region's aggregation name, child type, multiplicity, and any drag-and-drop declaration so tooling can reconstruct the layout contract without inspecting renderer code.
implementationNotes: Regions are the same aggregations defined by the component model; the layout system constrains their arrangement and adaptation rather than introducing a separate composition mechanism.
Responsive arrangement changes must be reversible and content-preserving: the same children resolve at every breakpoint, density, and theme, only their placement changes.
Density and spacing are sourced from a shared, theme-driven scale so that changing density or theme never alters which regions or children are present.
Drag-and-drop is constrained by the target aggregation's child type and multiplicity, so a reorder or cross-region move can never produce a composition that violates the component contract.

examples.title: Ordered and named composition regions example
examples.description: A page that declares 0..1 header and footer regions together with an ordered 0..n content region demonstrates named placement and a normative document order for owned children.

examples.title: Responsive grid across breakpoints example
examples.description: A grid that keeps the same ordered items aggregation while changing column count at compact, medium, and expanded breakpoints demonstrates responsive reflow that preserves the identity and order of owned items.

examples.title: Density and spacing scale example
examples.description: A form that exposes a density property selecting row gap and control height from a shared spacing scale demonstrates a layout-wide modifier that changes spacing without changing regions or children.

examples.title: Container drag-and-drop region example
examples.description: A board that declares drag-and-drop on its ordered columns aggregation demonstrates reordering constrained to the declared 0..n child type and multiplicity, keeping drag-and-drop within the component contract.

sections.title: Component Model
sections.purpose: Define the normative component contract.
requirements: Each component must expose a stable identity, owning library, supported interfaces, and public metadata.
Component metadata consists of properties, aggregations, associations, and events, each declared with a name, a type, and the modifiers that describe its public contract.

Properties declare typed configuration values with a data type, an optional default value, visibility, and an optional bindable flag; properties are the primary inputs that configure a component instance.

Aggregations declare owned child content with a child type, a multiplicity (0..1 or 0..n), visibility, and optional cardinality and binding semantics; aggregations express the parent-owned composition tree and lifecycle.

Associations declare non-owning references to other components by a target type and multiplicity; associations express semantic links such as labels, ARIA references, and related controls without transferring ownership.

Events declare named notifications that a component emits, together with the typed parameters delivered to listeners; events are the primary outputs through which a component reports interaction and state changes.
Visibility (public or hidden) and default values are part of the contract: only public metadata is normative, and declared defaults define the initial state when no value is supplied.
Components may additionally expose renderer linkage, design-time hooks, and drag-and-drop capabilities as optional, non-behavioral extensions of the contract.
nonGoals: The component model does not prescribe a specific renderer implementation, DOM structure, CSS strategy, or runtime framework.
The component model does not treat private or internal members as part of the contract; only public metadata is normative.
The component model does not require every component to declare every metadata category; a component may expose only the properties, aggregations, associations, or events that its contract needs.

The component model does not define the higher-level composition of pages, forms, or dialogs; those are described in the UI concept, layout, and form sections as specialized components built from this contract.

tags.name: component-identity
tags.meaning: Every component exposes a stable name and owning library that uniquely identify it within the public catalog.

tags.name: property-contract
tags.meaning: Properties are typed, optionally default-valued, visibility-scoped configuration inputs declared in metadata.

tags.name: aggregation-contract
tags.meaning: Aggregations are typed, multiplicity-constrained, parent-owned composition relationships declared in metadata.

tags.name: association-contract
tags.meaning: Associations are typed, multiplicity-constrained, non-owning references between components declared in metadata.

tags.name: event-contract
tags.meaning: Events are named, typed-parameter notifications a component emits to its listeners.

tags.name: visibility-and-defaults
tags.meaning: Visibility (public/hidden) and declared default values define which members are normative and what the initial state is.

tags.name: renderer-designtime-dnd
tags.meaning: Renderer linkage, design-time hooks, and drag-and-drop flags are optional capabilities layered onto the core metadata contract.

formalDefinitions.definition: A public, named unit published by a library that exposes a metadata contract of properties, aggregations, associations, and events.
A metadata member declaring a typed configuration value with a data type, optional default value, visibility, and optional bindable flag.
A metadata member declaring owned child content with a child type, multiplicity (0..1 or 0..n), and visibility, forming part of the parent's composition tree and lifecycle.
A metadata member declaring a non-owning reference to one or more components of a target type, used for semantic links rather than ownership.
A metadata member declaring a named notification, together with the typed parameters delivered to listeners when the component emits it.
The cardinality of an aggregation or association, expressed as 0..1 (single, multiple: false) or 0..n (collection, multiple: true).
The contract scope of a metadata member, public when it is part of the normative contract or hidden when it is excluded from it.
The value a property assumes when no explicit value is supplied, defining the initial state of a component instance.

usage: Use this model as the normative contract for every public component before applying domain-specific rules from the interaction, state, data-binding, form, or accessibility sections.
Declare configuration inputs as properties with explicit types and defaults; reserve aggregations for owned child content and associations for non-owning semantic references.
Express outputs as events with named, typed parameters so consumers can react to interaction and state changes without inspecting internal implementation.
Mark members that are not part of the public contract as hidden, and treat only public metadata as normative for validation, documentation, and generation.

When normalizing metadata into /openui.json, preserve each member's name, type, multiplicity, visibility, default value, and bindable flag so tooling can reconstruct the full component contract without reverse-engineering runtime code.
Treat renderer linkage, design-time hooks, and drag-and-drop flags as optional capability metadata that augments, but never replaces, the core property, aggregation, association, and event contract.
implementationNotes: Implementations should publish each component's metadata contract through a stable catalog so applications, validators, and generators can enumerate properties, aggregations, associations, and events without source inspection.

Public metadata should declare types, multiplicity, visibility, default values, and bindable flags explicitly so the component contract can be interpreted without relying on renderer or runtime internals.

Generator pipelines should map properties to inputs, aggregations to owned child slots, associations to non-owning references, and events to outputs, while treating renderer, design-time, and drag-and-drop metadata as optional capability hints.

examples.title: Component identity and properties example
examples.description: A button that declares its name, owning library, and supported interfaces together with typed text, enabled, and type properties (each with a default value and visibility) demonstrates stable identity and the property input contract.

examples.title: Aggregations and multiplicity example
examples.description: A panel that declares a 0..n content aggregation and a 0..1 header aggregation, with content as the default aggregation, demonstrates parent-owned composition with explicit multiplicity.

examples.title: Associations and events example
examples.description: An input that exposes a bindable value property, ariaLabelledBy and ariaDescribedBy associations, and change and liveChange events with typed parameters demonstrates non-owning references and typed outputs alongside property inputs.

examples.title: Renderer, design-time, and drag-and-drop example
examples.description: A card that augments its content aggregation with a renderer linkage, draggable and droppable flags, and a design-time reference demonstrates optional capability metadata that does not change the core property, aggregation, association, and event contract.

sections.title: Interaction Model
sections.purpose: Describe how user interaction is represented in the specification.
requirements: User-triggered behavior is modeled as named events with stable semantics, such as `press` for activation.
Interaction rules must account for enabled and disabled states, pointer activation, and keyboard activation.
Deprecated interaction aliases may be documented for compatibility but must not replace the current public event contract.
Interaction events describe component-level intent rather than raw browser gestures, so a single event can normalize pointer, touch, keyboard, and assistive-technology activation.
Event metadata must declare the event name, owning component, optional parameters, cancellation semantics, and the state preconditions that allow the event to fire.
Controls that expose action semantics must document how default activation keys and pointer gestures map to the public event while preserving accessibility expectations.
Application handlers attach to the public event contract and must not depend on private DOM events, renderer internals, or browser-specific event ordering.
nonGoals: The interaction model does not standardize private browser event listeners, renderer implementation details, or DOM event propagation order.
The interaction model does not require every component to expose an activation event; passive display controls may have no user-triggered behavior.
The interaction model does not replace accessibility, form validation, navigation, or feedback rules, but it supplies the event contract those sections can reference.

tags.name: semantic-interaction-event
tags.meaning: Public events describe framework-level user intent rather than raw DOM gestures.

tags.name: activation-event-contract
tags.meaning: Action controls expose a stable activation event such as `press` for application handlers.

tags.name: enabled-state-gating
tags.meaning: Disabled controls suppress public interaction events for pointer, touch, keyboard, and assistive activation paths.

tags.name: pointer-keyboard-equivalence
tags.meaning: Equivalent pointer and keyboard activation paths produce the same public event when the control is enabled.

tags.name: compatibility-alias
tags.meaning: Legacy or deprecated event names may be documented as aliases, but the current public event remains normative.

formalDefinitions.definition: A public component event that represents user-triggered intent in framework terms rather than a raw browser event.
An interaction event that represents intentional activation of an action control, commonly named `press` for button-like controls.
The input path that caused an interaction event, such as pointer click, touch tap, keyboard activation, or assistive technology action.
The state precondition that allows a control to translate an interaction source into a public interaction event.
A legacy event name or interaction term retained for documentation or migration while delegating normative behavior to the current event contract.

usage: Use interaction events when documenting application-observable behavior, and reserve raw DOM event names for implementation notes or test fixtures.
Define action controls around a single public activation event so applications can handle pointer, touch, keyboard, and assistive activation consistently.
Check enabled and disabled state before dispatching public interaction events; disabled controls must not call application handlers through alternate activation paths.
Preserve event parameter names, types, optionality, and cancellation semantics in `/openui.json` so generators and tests can create stable handler signatures.
Document compatibility aliases only when they are part of the supported public surface, and point consumers to the current event name for new implementations.
implementationNotes: Extractors should normalize public interaction metadata from control event declarations and avoid treating private renderer listeners as normative events.
Generators should bind application handlers through the target framework activation mechanism while preserving the specification enabled gate and handler signature.
Conformance tests should cover pointer activation, keyboard activation, disabled-state suppression, and any documented compatibility aliases for action controls.

examples.title: Button activation event contract example
examples.description: A button declares `press` as the public activation event, normalizing pointer, touch, keyboard, and assistive activation sources behind one handler contract.

examples.title: Enabled-state gating example
examples.description: A disabled button suppresses public activation events even when a pointer or keyboard source attempts to activate the control.

examples.title: Generator output handler binding example
examples.description: A generator output maps the activation event to the target framework handler binding while preserving the enabled gate so disabled controls do not invoke application logic.

sections.title: State Model
sections.purpose: Describe state held or exposed by components.
requirements: Component state is represented by typed properties with explicit default values.
State may be public or hidden, but only public state participates in the external component contract.
Defaulted state and derived state must remain compatible with the declared metadata type system.
State-related metadata must preserve property type, default value, visibility, and deprecation information so generators can produce safe accessors and validators.
Hidden or derived implementation state must not be used as the only source for application-visible behavior when an equivalent public property is declared.
nonGoals: The state model does not define data-binding syntax, binding lifecycle, or model update timing; those rules belong to the data binding model.
The state model does not prescribe renderer internals, DOM attributes, or CSS classes used to realize a public state.
The state model does not require hidden state to be documented as part of the public API.

tags.name: metadata-property-state
tags.meaning: Component state is declared through metadata properties rather than inferred from private fields.

tags.name: explicit-default-state
tags.meaning: Every normalized state property records the value used before applications set a different value.

tags.name: public-state-contract
tags.meaning: Public state defines the accessor, binding, documentation, and compatibility surface exposed to applications.

tags.name: hidden-internal-state
tags.meaning: Hidden state may support implementation details but does not become part of the external component contract.

tags.name: derived-state-compatibility
tags.meaning: Computed or normalized state remains valid only when it is compatible with the declared property type and default.

formalDefinitions.definition: The observable configuration or condition of a component represented by metadata-declared properties and their current values.
Component state with public visibility, including properties that omit an explicit visibility value when the metadata grammar treats public as the default.
Metadata-declared state marked as hidden or otherwise internal so it can be used by implementations without becoming application-facing API.
The initial value for a state property before application code, binding, or framework logic assigns another compatible value.
State computed from one or more declared values, such as normalized enabled, visible, or validation state, that must remain type-compatible with the public contract it reflects.

usage: Use typed properties for component states such as `text`, `enabled`, `visible`, `selected`, `value`, `valueState`, `busy`, or `width` whenever those states are part of the public contract.
Preserve explicit defaults when normalizing metadata into `/openui.json`; if source metadata relies on a framework default, record the resolved value used by conforming implementations.
Treat `visibility: "hidden"` state as implementation detail: generators may keep it out of public APIs, documentation, and examples unless a compliance rule explicitly requires otherwise.
Validate default values, assigned values, and derived values against the declared metadata type so boolean, string, enum, numeric, object, and array states remain predictable.
Keep state semantics separate from events: a state property describes the current condition, while events describe the interaction or lifecycle transitions that may change it.
implementationNotes: Extraction pipelines should preserve property names, declared types, default values, visibility, and deprecation metadata when creating the canonical state model.
Generator pipelines should expose public state through typed inputs, accessors, bindings, or equivalent framework constructs while omitting hidden state from generated public APIs.
Validation tooling should verify that defaults and derived state values conform to the declared metadata type and that hidden state is not the only documented source for externally visible behavior.

examples.title: Public typed state with defaults example
examples.description: A button declares public text, enabled, visible, and type properties with explicit defaults so applications and generators share the same initial state contract.

examples.title: Hidden internal state example
examples.description: A tokenizer keeps selected as public state while marking an internal measurement property hidden so tooling does not expose rendering support data as API.

examples.title: Derived validation state example
examples.description: An input derives effective validation state from required, value, and valueState properties while preserving the declared ValueState enum type.

sections.title: Data Binding Model
sections.purpose: Describe how component state binds to external models.
requirements: Bindable properties and aggregations are part of the component contract and must be explicitly declared with a bindable flag.

Bindings may target scalar properties or compositional collections depending on the metadata kind: property bindings connect a single typed value, while aggregation bindings connect a model collection to an owned aggregation through a template.
Every binding resolves against a model and an addressable path within that model; a binding may use the default model or a named model.
Async or reactive model updates must preserve the declared property and aggregation types so bound values stay compatible with the component contract.
Members that are not declared bindable, or that are hidden, are not part of the public binding contract and must not be relied on as binding targets.
nonGoals: The data binding model does not standardize a single model implementation, query language, or transport protocol such as JSON, OData, or REST.
The data binding model does not define expression-language grammar, formatter syntax, or two-way change-detection internals; it only requires that updates preserve declared types.
The data binding model does not describe form-level validation or submission semantics, which belong to the form model, nor renderer or DOM details used to display bound values.

tags.name: bindable-contract
tags.meaning: Only metadata members explicitly declared bindable participate in the public data binding contract.

tags.name: property-binding
tags.meaning: A property binding connects a single addressable model value to one typed, scalar property.

tags.name: aggregation-binding
tags.meaning: An aggregation binding connects a model collection to an owned aggregation by repeating a template for each entry.

tags.name: binding-path-context
tags.meaning: Every binding resolves a path against a default or named model, optionally relative to a binding context.

tags.name: type-preserving-updates
tags.meaning: Async or reactive model updates must keep bound values compatible with the declared property and aggregation types.

formalDefinitions.definition: A declared connection between a bindable component member and a value or collection addressed in a model.
An external, addressable source of state that a binding reads from and, for two-way bindings, writes back to.
The address, optionally qualified by a model name and relative to a binding context, that selects the value or collection a binding targets.
A binding whose target is a single scalar property and whose value must remain compatible with the declared property type.
A binding whose target is an owned aggregation, expanding a model collection into child instances through a declared template while preserving the aggregation's child type and multiplicity.

usage: Declare a property or aggregation as bindable in metadata before treating it as a binding target; non-bindable and hidden members are not part of the binding contract.

Bind scalar configuration values, such as `text`, `value`, or `selected`, with property bindings, and bind owned collections, such as `items` or `content`, with aggregation bindings driven by a template.
Qualify each binding path with the default model or an explicit named model, and resolve relative paths against the active binding context so list templates address per-entry values.

Preserve the declared metadata type when normalizing bindings into `/openui.json`: record the bound member, its kind (property or aggregation), the bindable flag, the model, and the path so generators can reconstruct the binding.

Keep binding semantics separate from interaction and state: a binding describes where a value comes from, while events describe transitions and state describes the current condition, and async updates must not change the declared type.
implementationNotes: Extraction pipelines should record the bindable flag on properties and aggregations, the model name (default or named), the binding path, and the binding kind so the canonical model captures the full binding contract.

Generator pipelines should map property bindings to typed framework inputs or bindings and aggregation bindings to template-driven repetition over a typed collection, omitting non-bindable and hidden members from generated binding APIs.

Validation tooling should verify that bound scalar values stay compatible with the declared property type, that aggregation bindings preserve the child type and multiplicity, and that async updates never violate the declared types.

examples.title: Scalar property binding example
examples.description: An input binds its scalar text value to a single model path so the bound value stays compatible with the declared string property type.

examples.title: Aggregation list binding example
examples.description: A list binds an owned items aggregation to a model collection, expanding a template for each entry while preserving the child type and multiplicity.

examples.title: Type-preserving async update example
examples.description: A named model delivers an asynchronous update that keeps the bound property compatible with its declared type so the component contract is never violated.

sections.title: Form Model
sections.purpose: Describe form-oriented components and semantics.
requirements: Form-compatible components may advertise dedicated interfaces such as form-content contracts.
Form semantics require explicit label and description relationships through associations or equivalent metadata.
Validation and submission behavior must be representable through public properties, events, and feedback components.

sections.title: Navigation Model
sections.purpose: Describe the navigation structures the framework must support.
requirements: The framework must support navigable page containers, dialogs, and shell-level composition primitives.
Navigation state must be expressible as public component state and events rather than hidden renderer behavior.
Route-aware or deep-linkable components should be discoverable from the public component catalog.

sections.title: Feedback Model
sections.purpose: Define how user-visible feedback is modeled.
requirements: The framework must expose components for busy, message, dialog, and empty-state feedback patterns.
Feedback components must be addressable through the same public metadata contract as all other components.
Action completion, failure, and warning scenarios must be representable through public events and state.

sections.title: Accessibility Model
sections.purpose: Capture accessibility requirements visible in the public contract.
requirements: Components must support accessible naming and description relationships through public associations or equivalent metadata.
Accessibility roles, popup semantics, and text direction must be declarable as typed public state when applicable.
Keyboard activation and focus behavior are compliance-relevant parts of the interaction contract.

sections.title: Theming / Design Tokens
sections.purpose: Describe visual customization requirements.
requirements: Libraries must support theme-specific assets and shared theme-level styling resources.
Theme composition must allow base styles plus theme overrides without changing the public component contract.
Density-aware and theme-aware assets are part of the visual system and must remain externally configurable.

sections.title: Internationalization
sections.purpose: Describe locale and translation requirements.
requirements: Libraries must provide translatable message resources for public UI text.
Locale-aware rendering must support right-to-left and left-to-right presentation where applicable.
Public text-bearing components must expose text direction and translatable labels through explicit metadata.

sections.title: Security / Privacy UI Rules
sections.purpose: State UI-facing security and privacy constraints.
requirements: Public component contracts must not rely on unsafe renderer side effects for user-visible semantics.
Components that open or reference popups must declare that relationship explicitly through public metadata.
Implementations should encode or sanitize user-provided visual values before they affect rendering or styling.

sections.title: Performance Requirements
sections.purpose: Describe performance-oriented expectations that follow from the public model.
requirements: Public library catalogs should allow eager discovery of available components without eager loading every implementation detail.
Machine-readable API projections should be derivable from the same public contract used by runtime metadata.
Visual assets and behavior should support lazy or conditional loading when the public contract permits it.

sections.title: Extension Model
sections.purpose: Describe supported extension points.
requirements: The framework must allow new controls, elements, types, and interfaces to be introduced through the same catalog and metadata model.
Design-time metadata may extend authoring semantics without redefining the runtime component contract.
Extension points must preserve compatibility with theming, accessibility, and interaction rules.

sections.title: Compliance Rules
sections.purpose: Summarize what every implementation must satisfy.
requirements: A compliant implementation must publish its public surface through a discoverable library catalog.
A compliant implementation must describe every public component with typed metadata for properties, composition, relationships, and events.
A compliant implementation must preserve accessibility, theming, internationalization, and testing evidence as part of the public contract lifecycle.

sections.title: Test & Acceptance Criteria
sections.purpose: Describe how the specification should be verified.
requirements: Every public metadata contract should be verifiable by automated tests that exercise properties, events, and rendering-facing semantics.
Acceptance criteria should cover unit behavior, visual states, accessibility semantics, and responsive or theme-sensitive presentation.
Machine-readable projections such as `api.json` should remain consistent with runtime metadata and documented behavior.

examples.title: UI concept model acceptance test
examples.description: A compliant test should verify that published controls and elements are classified correctly, owned UI regions remain aggregations, semantic references remain associations, and higher-level concepts such as dialogs and forms are represented through metadata-defined composition.

examples.title: Application structure acceptance test
examples.description: A compliant test should verify that every component referenced by the application structure resolves to a declared library dependency, the shell exposes navigation and owned pages through aggregations, non-owning references such as the current page remain associations, and the nested page hierarchy is resolvable from public metadata.

examples.title: Layout system acceptance test
examples.description: Given a normalized specification entry for a Page, Grid, Form, and Board layout contract: verify that named regions such as header, content, and footer are aggregations with declared multiplicity and child types; verify that ordered 0..n regions preserve their declared document order as the normative visual order before responsive adaptation; verify that responsive breakpoints change region arrangement without adding, removing, or reparenting owned content; verify that density and spacing are drawn from a shared, theme-driven scale instead of ad-hoc measurements; and verify that drag-and-drop is present only where a container declares dnd metadata and stays within the target aggregation's child type and multiplicity.

examples.title: Component model acceptance test
examples.description: A compliant test should verify that each component exposes a stable identity, that properties declare types and default values, that aggregations declare child types and multiplicity, that associations remain non-owning references, that events declare typed parameters, and that renderer, design-time, and drag-and-drop metadata stay optional capability hints rather than part of the core contract.

sections.title: Reference Examples
sections.purpose: Provide a concrete reference component abstracted from upstream OpenUI5 evidence.
requirements: A reference action component exposes text, style, width, enabled state, icon configuration, accessibility state, and a primary activation event.
The same component may expose label/description associations, drag capability, and theme-aware visuals.
Its expected behavior is validated by tests for state accessors, text direction, activation events, and visibility.
